intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, animated progress indicators over static percentage text when tracking health experiments
facade.label: Smooth progress bars
facade.acceptImplies: Design emphasizes kinetic feedback—movements, animations, and transitions as primary feedback mechanisms rather than numeric readouts
facade.rejectImplies: Design favors instant, static information hierarchy—charts, numbers, and fixed layouts over motion-based communication

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, animated progress indicators over static percentage text when tracking health experiments
evidence.implication: Design emphasizes kinetic feedback—movements, animations, and transitions as primary feedback mechanisms rather than numeric readouts

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards benefit from organic, approachable forms over rigid geometry
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers friendly, less clinical feel; design should use curves, soft buttons, gentle edges
facade.rejectImplies: User gravitates toward modern minimalism or clinical clarity; sharp corners and clean lines are acceptable or preferred

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a conversational, encouragement-focused tone over clinical health terminology
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: Embrace warm, familiar language; use celebratory micro-copy; friendly notifications and feedback
facade.rejectImplies: Lean into clinical precision; use medical/scientific terms; neutral, data-first framing; minimize personality

facade.hypothesis: Users want to filter experiments by category (left sidebar) while viewing detailed experiment cards in a scrollable main area
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into hierarchical nav patterns—category filters on left, rich card previews on right. User values quick filtering + contextual detail.
facade.rejectImplies: Try flat tab-based navigation or list-view instead. User may prefer linear browsing or minimal sidebar clutter.

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards benefit from rolled-up weekly views over daily granularity
facade.axisTargeted: AGGREGATION LEVEL — daily detail vs. weekly summary vs. monthly overview
facade.label: Weekly summary cards
facade.acceptImplies: User wants time-compressed trends; favor card layouts with week-over-week comparison, fewer data points, narrative takeaways
facade.rejectImplies: User needs daily precision and drill-down; favor dense tables, day-by-day rows, full historical access, minimal summarization

facade.hypothesis: A dashboard with sparse, card-based layout using negative space and typography-first design will feel calm and focused rather than overwhelming, supporting sustained long-term tracking without fatigue.
facade.label: Minimalist data cards
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward uncluttered interfaces; values breathing room; may prefer text + numbers over visualizations; likely wants restraint in color and animation.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants density of information visible at once; may find sparse layouts feel empty or slow; likely prefers rich visuals, charts, or more visual stimulation; wants to feel the data's presence.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid gesture-based input (swipe, drag, shake) over click-based logging when recording daily health data, reducing friction in repetitive tracking tasks
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — gesture-driven interaction vs click-based interaction; continuous motion vs discrete taps
facade.label: Gesture swipe for logging
facade.acceptImplies: Design favors kinetic, embodied input methods that reward smooth hand movements and provide immediate tactile/visual feedback, prioritizing flow over precision
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer fast, deliberate taps or button presses; gesture-based logging adds friction or feels imprecise for health data; logging should be invisible/backgrounded rather than interactive

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
